data.title: The 'No Pressure' Ceasefire
data.description:
The market is off the charts. The S&P 500 (SP500) closed at a new record high multiple times. The U.S. and Iran are still in talks to end the war, the first round of which failed last weekend, putting more pressure on reaching common ground in this next round of talks, which Iran has yet to confirm it will take part in. It’s without a doubt that the market is pricing the glass half full, meaning eyeing a soon-to-be resolution. Oil futures (CL1:COM, CO1:COM) are doing the same: even with a surge this morning, oil remains below $100 per barrel in the paper market, while it's trading closer to $145 in the spot, or physical, market.

Bjarne Schieldrop, chief commodities analyst at SEB AB, noted that

"markets are still betting on a timely resolution, but each day raises shortage risk…Physical oil flows remain constrained by disrupted flows, longer voyage times, and elevated freight and insurance costs."

All signals point to the market not panicking, but the “ceasefire” rally has dangerously pushed stocks past pre-war levels. The worst part, arguably, is that this is a ceasefire in which, as of this weekend, fire has not ceased.

The Open Strait lasted less than 24 hours

The U.S. and Iran are back in the red zone after a great end to last week, with Iran officially opening the Strait of Hormuz. The U.S. refused to remove its own naval blockade, which went into effect a week ago today.

The U.S. forced about 23 vessels back to Iran as part of its blockade, according to Reuters. Iran reinstated its own blockade in response, with Iran’s Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf stating: “It is impossible for others to pass through the Strait of Hormuz while we cannot.” Things got messier when the U.S. and Iran got more confrontational over the weekend, with the latter reportedly firing at tankers and the U.S. Navy seizing an Iranian vessel. Traffic through the Strait is halted once again, with Friday’s opening lasting less than 24 hours. Trump’s post on Truth Social describing the takeover read as follows:

So this places us in a ceasefire in which the Strait remains closed and the global oil supply disruption deepens. The chart below showcases how Gulf producers lost 40% of their output last month.

According to Phillip Cornell, a senior fellow at the Atlantic Council’s Global Energy Center based out of Sri Lanka, “The impacts are so rapid and deep… Just from a magnitude perspective, this is really very, very, very large.” This isn’t an exaggerated concern; instead, it’s an underrated risk from our viewpoint. The impact of this will be sticky even if the war is resolved. We’re looking at 500M barrels that have been removed from the market, equivalent to cutting global aviation demand for 10 weeks, a month of oil demand in the U.S., or six years of fuel consumption for the U.S. military. We’re not talking pennies. It’ll take time to bring the global market back into equilibrium. The chart below shows the change in global oil inventories on an M/M basis, highlighting the buildup since the war began.

Haris Khurshid, CIO at Karobaar Capital, explained the situation, saying:

The market’s still carrying a risk premium into the deadline, but just not fully committing to it…If things just continue as they are, you probably see a gradual push higher to around $105–$115, but with a lot of back and forth on headlines.

Who’s coming to Islamabad?

The 14-day ceasefire is almost at an end, with the deadline approaching this Wednesday. Officials are expected to meet in Islamabad, Pakistan, to make a deal. We know the U.S. is coming to the table led by VP JD Vance, special envoy Steve Witkoff, and Jared Kushner. Trump is giving mixed signals; on one hand, saying there is hope for a solid deal while standing ready for the use of force. Meanwhile, the Iranians are signaling that there is no “clear prospect” for a deal. The fundamental problem with the talks at the moment is that Iran may not show up to any. Iranian state TV reported the following statement:

U.S. overreach, unreasonable and unrealistic demands, frequent changes in positions, continuous contradictions, and the continuation of the so-called naval blockade, which is considered a violation of the ceasefire agreement, along with threatening rhetoric, have so far hindered the progress of the negotiations

The confrontation in Lebanon, which entered into a 10-day ceasefire last week, also appears to be growing more fragile. On Saturday, Israel attacked targets in South Lebanon, and Israeli troops remain present in the area. So there is a lot of confusion stemming from contradictory statements from both sides and the status of the current ceasefire. Trump posted the following on Truth Social this Sunday, taking the conversation back to where it was pre-ceasefire with renewed threats on Iran’s power plants and bridges.

The other side of the coin is that Europe is growing more fearful of where these talks are headed. The concern, according to a Reuters report, is that “Washington's negotiating team is pushing for a swift, superficial deal that would require months or years of technically complex follow‑on talks.” The theme is uncertainty, and Wednesday should help bring much-needed clarity on where this face-off is headed next and how seriously markets should take the lack of progress in the talks over the past two weeks.

Earnings season complicates the rally

We’re heading into the thickest week for tech earnings next week, with about one-fifth of the entire S&P 500 expected to report. All tier 1 players are reporting on the same day, next Wednesday, and have all handsomely recovered over the past month, as shown below. The average gain for a member of the Mag7 has been 11% over the past month as the AI story picks back up.

The context going into next week matters. Investors are leaning heavily into tech rather than away into safer areas of investment. The S&P 500 not only broke all-time highs propped up by the Mag 7, but also saw an unprecedented bounce back from its wartime low in March, sliding 9% from its January high before recovering 12% from its late March low. Bespoke Investment Group studied S&P 500 pullbacks of 5% to 10% and emphasized that a recovery into new all-time highs over a handful of sessions had never happened before last Wednesday. Given how the market has moved post-ceasefire, surpassing pre-war levels, earnings must deliver because everyone is already pricing in a best-case scenario.

We expect this earnings season to be a mixed bag, and investors, not unlike in Q1, will still be harder to please. We're back at the tug of war conversation between greed and fear. While we expect investors to be quicker to take profit during earnings on a slip-up post-rally, we are simultaneously expecting to see a lot of bottom fishing. We continue to see fundamentally driven investments as the way to navigate this playing field and reaffirm our sentiment on analog, with Texas Instruments (TXN) being the first of the bunch to report later this week on Wednesday. We expect positive benefits from price hikes and double ordering due to supply uncertainty.

Another positive data point in favor of the analog peer group, especially those with heavier auto exposure, is the Pentagon's approach to auto manufacturers to help boost weapon production. We view analog as having a favorable risk-reward profile, even if we see a longer ceasefire or a war dragging out. We expect TXN management to shed more light on how the industry is digesting the situation. We already got some color from TSMC (TSM), which we’ve flagged as especially exposed to current disruptions, given that Taiwan imports about 70% of its crude oil and a third of its LNG from the Middle East. CFO Jen-Chau Huang noted the following:

In terms of energy, TSMC worked closely with Taipower and the Taiwan government to ensure a stable and sufficient energy supply. With the recent situation in the Middle East, the Taiwan government has announced it has secured sufficient LNG supply through at least May. The government has also said it is actively working on securing further LNG supply, diversifying sourcing to other regions and other power backup plants. Therefore, we do not expect any near-term disruption or impact to our operations.

We expect that on the semi supply chain side of things, the current situation will be a point of conversation. We also expect the current backdrop to reflect on discretionary spending if we see uncertainty stretch out longer than Wednesday’s deadline, particularly with gas prices hitting $4 a gallon and not expected to return to sub-$3 levels until 2027, according to U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright. Trump has since come out to dismiss Wright’s statement, calling it “Totally wrong” and instead suggesting that costs will be lower as soon as the war ends.

The ceasefire will prove the market right if we get a deal by the end of this week. Our sense is that we're going to see the conversation stretched out longer than either side would like. That said, we think it'll take a dramatic end to the ceasefire to knock markets back into reality. We'll keep you posted in next week's macro rundown.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Two Key Indicators Are Pointing To Higher Prices Into Fall
data.description:
This is a continuation of the educational series on investor sentiment and how to measure and apply it in today’s market.

Warren Buffett said, “A simple rule dictates my buying. Be fearful when others are greedy and be greedy when others are fearful.” Notice he didn’t say “interest rates” or “earnings,” just “emotions.” This was his way of stating the theory of contrary opinion. You get "greed" when everyone is bullish and "fear" when prices are plunging and they're all bearish.

The Theory Of Contrary Opinion

The theory of contrary opinion is very simple. It says that when “too many” investors think stock prices will advance, they usually decline. Likewise, when “too many” think prices will decline, they advance. In other words, prices move opposite to what investors expect when those expectations have reached an extreme.

Why The Theory Works

There’s an accepted reason why the theory works so well. It’s simple but a little counterintuitive.

Buying drives prices higher. But if everyone is bullish, the majority of investors have already purchased, which means there is no one left to buy to push prices higher. So now just a little normal selling moves them lower.

Similarly, if everyone is bearish, most people have sold their stocks, so no one is left to sell to push prices lower. So just a little buying moves prices higher.

To apply the theory, you need indicators that measure what investors expect.

Indicators That Measure Investor Expectations

While Buffett mentioned fear and greed, he didn’t provide ways to measure them. We follow 18 indicators that measure investor expectations in various ways. They’re listed below. Our subscribers get a weekly analysis of all of them, plus those for individual stocks.

Many of these indicators rely on actions of certain types or classes of investors, such as option traders or short sellers. It's important to understand that these classes of investors don't drive the market but provide statistical samples of what investors who do drive the market are thinking or doing.

The two we are going to review in this article – number 5 and number 13 - are highlighted in red. We use The Red Zone, Green Zone ranking system to evaluate the indicators.

The Red Zone, Green Zone Ranking

We use a Red Zone - Green Zone ranking system to determine when an indicator shows extreme bullish or bearish expectations. This is done by comparing current numbers to historic norms from many years back. The red zone represents extreme bullish expectations, while the Green Zone represents extreme bearish expectations. Remember it’s used as a contrary opinion indicator.

ProShare Ultra Short to Long Buying

This indicator shows that ProShare investors were putting near record amounts into short funds compared to long funds, expecting lower prices. This is very bullish. As you can see from the chart, Green Zone signals are usually followed by intermediate to long term movements in the market. In other words, the indicated rally should last at least into late summer or autumn.

The AAII Survey

This indicator shows that members of the American Association of Individual Investors are historically very bearish. The survey represents their expectations over the next six months, so it's not a short term survey.

While it's not at the extremes that occurred in 2020, or the bear market lows of 2022, or even last April’s low, it's high enough to expect a significant rally.

Summary

We've explained two indicators – The ProShares Ultra short to long buying ratio and the American Association of Individual Investors member survey – that indicate a high level of bearish expectations among investors. Historically, this is bullish, and it's bullish for the intermediate to long term. It's not a short term indicator.

We think they're forecasting the long-awaited second wave of the movement that started last April and believe it will carry on into late 2026.

data.title: NerdWallet: Despite Search Challenges, The Bottom Line Remains Strong
data.description:
We're heading into the beginning of the Q1 earnings season, and the stock market is hovering around all-time highs even though we have no concrete resolution yet on the war in Iran. In my view, this is a time to be incredibly cautious, especially with large-cap stocks sitting at heavy valuations. The small- and mid-cap space, however, is still rife with bargain opportunities after major slides this year.

NerdWallet (NRDS), the personal finance website, has seen a ~15% erosion in its share price to date, erasing much of last year's gains (and relative to highs near $16 that the stock notched as recently as December, the stock is down ~30%, technically putting it in a bear market).

Is NerdWallet a legacy company that is bound to keep losing value, or is it a hidden "growth at a reasonable price" company that can stage an upward re-rating of its very low valuation multiples?

I last wrote a "Buy" opinion on NerdWallet in January, when the stock was trading near $13 per share. While the decline in share price since then has been frustrating, NerdWallet has actually released good news since then. Even though the company is still facing pressure from organic search traffic that is reducing revenue in some key legacy verticals like credit cards, the company is making up for it with affiliate revenue from other high-growth sources (insurance, brokerage accounts).

Also, increasingly small- and mid-cap investors are becoming more sensitive to "quality of earnings" when companies report adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow, particularly regarding how large stock-based comp add-backs are as a chunk of these adjusted earnings metrics. Relative to both its earnings power and its market cap, NerdWallet's stock-based comp dilution is relatively low, which underpins the company's healthy current growth rates. While I can't argue that growth headwinds don't exist, I find these to be well priced in, and I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on this stock.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to this stock, here are the core reasons to be long on NerdWallet:

  • Diversified suite of finance content and revenue streams. The company is an all-in-one suite of financial advice and content for both individuals (personal finance) and small businesses. The company generates revenue from credit cards, bank accounts, insurance, mortgage lending, brokerages, financial advisors, and others. While search traffic may impact some categories (like credit cards), the company has more evergreen revenue streams that are making up for it.
  • Huge gross margin profile. The company has a tiny cost of revenue, leading to a mid-90% gross margin profile. This gives the company plenty of room to chase leads via performance marketing, and enables substantial economies of scale.
  • Strong balance sheet. The company has $98.3 million of cash on its books, completely free of debt, which gives a lot of financial flexibility to a small-cap company.

Of course, valuation remains one of the core reasons to remain bullish on this stock as we hope for an upward re-rating. At current share prices near $11, NerdWallet trades at a $730.9 million market cap. Netting off the $98.3 million of cash on NerdWallet's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of just $632.6 million.

For FY26, as shown above, the company has guided to adjusted EBITDA of $143-$158 million in FY25, the $150.5 million midpoint of which reflects 4% y/y growth. Wall Street is worried about the implied deceleration here (Q1 revenue guidance calls for just 9% y/y growth, representing more than 10 points of sequential deceleration) as the company faces distinct headwinds from tough y/y comps in insurance plus fading organic search traffic. Despite this, we find these risks to be priced at just 4.2x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA.

As previously mentioned, investors have begun to scrutinize stock-based comp more, especially in high-flying Silicon Valley stocks. This is less of a concern for NerdWallet, where stock-based comp of $28.6 million for the full year FY25 represented less than 20% of adjusted EBITDA, and is a modest ~4% of the company's current market cap. In short, we think despite growth headwinds, NerdWallet's earnings quality is high. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through NerdWallet's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

NerdWallet's revenue grew 23% y/y to $225.4 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $211.0 million (+15% y/y) by a huge eight-point margin. The chart below, meanwhile, showcases that NerdWallet's organic revenue growth accelerated by a sharp 13 points y/y versus just 10% growth in Q3.

NerdWallet itself had also guided to just 15% y/y growth for Q4, and ended up overachieving to the tune of eight points. This is good context to keep in mind as we assess the pessimistic sentiment around NerdWallet's Q1 outlook, calling for a declaration to 9% y/y growth. NerdWallet is not an enterprise business; its revenue fluctuates based on clicks and affiliate revenue driven by in-quarter traffic. The company's guidance philosophy has historically taken on a conservative bent.

Of course, we cannot ignore the risks that the company faces, particularly from organic search traffic. The company has cited that as more users turn to AI chatbots instead of Google search, its organic inbound site visits have reduced. For now, the company notes that it has been offsetting weaker organic/SEO traffic with a boost to performance marketing. Per CFO Jun Lee's remarks on the Q&A portion of the Q4 earnings call:

We're focused on adding NGOI dollars as we discussed. So given the mix shift changes in performance marketing and organic, revenue tends to be not as correlated to NGOI and free cash flow and focusing on margin percentage targets would be limiting for our flexibility as we need to make the right economic decisions for our shareholders.

And so what -- it is true that what you have seen is correct where we are experiencing a decline in organic revenue, but we have been, at least from a revenue perspective, more than offsetting that with our performance marketing revenue. And in order to -- and what I would guide to is, I think you could really take a look at our performance marketing spend trend over the last couple of years. And I think that will give you a pretty good sense of how to think about our revenue growth from a performance marketing standpoint in the outer years. But at the moment, we're not guiding specifically to revenue channels.

We can see this already in the data, with NerdWallet's sales and marketing spend as a percentage of revenue rising to 72%, driven by a jump in performance marketing (which is geared to driving clicks), with a corresponding partial offset from a reduction in brand marketing (which focuses more on brand positioning and storytelling rather than driving specific actions and clicks).

The good news here is that, as previously mentioned, NerdWallet has a high mid-90% gross margin to be able to afford heavy performance marketing spend. Also, the company is seeing a strong response to advertising in certain product categories, like personal loans (revenue up 141% y/y to $42.3 million, now 19% of the company total) and banking/investment products (+57% y/y to $52.9 million, 24% of revenue).

NerdWallet is also driving offsetting expense rationalization in other opex items, cutting R&D spend to 7% of revenue (down -2 points y/y). As a result of this, the company still managed to grow adjusted EBITDA at 19% y/y to $36.7 million, reflecting a 16% adjusted EBITDA margin that peeled back just 1 point y/y.

The company's free cash flow for FY25 also grew 142% y/y to $117.8 million:

Key takeaways

With strong recent revenue trends that substantially outpaced the company's current guidance, it's not all doom and gloom for NerdWallet as we approach the start of the Q1 earnings season. Rich profit margins (which don't rely on a huge SBC add-back) give NerdWallet plenty of flexibility to leverage performance marketing to offset lower Google search traffic; the company could also develop direct integrations with AI chatbots to recover that traffic in the near future. This stock remains a compelling "Buy" at ~4x adjusted EBITDA.

data.title: Broadcom: Things Might Look Rosy, But I'm Not Buying The Growth Story
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Broadcom (AVGO) has had a fantastic past five years with it being able to rapidly scale its topline through both efficient and effective acquisitions, most notably VMWare which is the industry standard for virtual servers. But the narrative has shifted for a company focusing on M&A and more towards a company now supplying the data transfer between GPUs and data centers with networking chips.

It looks to be a first move market right now and AVGO has the headstart. I can see the comparison to NVIDIA (NVDA) a few years ago, and the market seems to see the same. Whether AVGO can live up to the hype will come down to whether it can establish the same dominant market position as NVDA has so far. I think it’s very unlikely when competition is now coming from every angle, and even possibly a long-term customer and its own partnering company NVDA. I’m skeptical of the market enthusiasm right now. Besides, the valuation seems stretched and some of the capital allocations raise questions too. I'm rating AVGO a Hold for now.

Operational Overview

AVGO is a chipmaker but differs quite a bit from the ones people normally think of, like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) or NVDA. Production wise, or lack thereof is similar. AVGO is a fabless chipmaker but rather than focusing on making the chips that power the “AI renaissance”, it instead works more with the networking side. This is the part where the massive amount of data being processed in data centers is used to transfer and send it. AVGO is the airline for data centers I guess could be a crude way of looking at this. At least this is where the main market consensus seems to be at right now.

AVGO is one of the best examples of what happens when a company invests heavily in building out its portfolio and not being scared of issuing debt to fund top line growth through acquisitions. People were rightly concerned when AVGO acquired VMWare in 2023 for $61 billion, leading to a now total debt pile of $66 billion.

So I’ve already mentioned the two main parts of AVGO right now. The networking chips are the crown jewel in the first segment, Semiconductor Solutions and VMWare are the largest part of the Infrastructure Software segment. By far the fastest growing segment is the first one. In Q1 FY2026 AVGO reported 52% YoY growth rate, but then forecasted that it would accelerate to 76% in Q2 this year with the combined segment revenues being $14.8 billion.

Today, in fact, we have line of sight to achieve AI revenue from chips, just chips, in excess of $100 billion in 2027. We have also secured the supply chain required to achieve this.

Considering then that the management of AVGO is also guiding for $100 billion in revenues from chips alone by 2027 tells a serious growth story. What was once a pretty slow growing company at the core, only really growing the top line through acquisitions and market consolidation is now one of the fastest growing tech companies in the world.

Why I’m skeptical still of the prospects for AVGO is the massive competition it has. For the moment it does have a certain first-move advantage. Similar to what NVDA had in the AI chip market, leading to both AMD and Intel (INTC) having to play catch up instead.

The main threat seems to be from Marvell Technology (MRVL), NVDA and Cisco Systems (CSCO) right now. Despite NVIDIA being a partner, it would seem odd that it would ignore such a large market opportunity as networking chips.

Marvell has also grown at a rapid pace the past few weeks after management cited continued growth. Although, I have to say, it’s a lot less than AVGO with only 8% sequential growth between quarters, leading to $3 billion by Q4. In total yearly revenues you’d be looking at something between $11-$12 billion. Then also consider that MRVL has a market cap of $126 billion compared to AVGO’s $1.92 trillion. Cash piles make a difference too, AVGO has $14.1 billion compared to $2.6 billion. But on the contrary, MRVL is not that deep in debt right now and could certainly afford to add more if it meant taking market share from AVGO.

The bigger fish that AVGO bulls would have to worry about is NVDA. Back in 2023 it launched an ethernet platform with Spectrum-X. Then last year in March it announced Spectrum-X Photonics which works with co-packaged optics that enables networking scaling to millions of GPUs across data centers. This is in direct competition with AVGO, and for anyone who has looked at the financials of NVDA, the pockets are even deeper than AVGO with cash sitting at $62 billion total debts at only $11 billion.

The last one is CSCO which is a bit odd seeing as it’s been a longstanding customer to Broadcom, and continues to be. But how long this will last is anyone's guess. Mine is that it won't be very long. This will of course have a negative impact on AVGO's revenue. The reason for the shift comes from CSCO now developing its own proprietary networking chips. The recent launch of Silicon One is just this. This makes it a direct challenger to AVGO.

Cisco is positioned for its strongest year ever as indicated in our guidance for fiscal year 2026, which is as follows. We expect revenue to be in the range of $61.2 billion to $61.7 billion.

Again, it’s a much smaller company than AVGO, but like the other two companies, guidance has been positive. The figure implies that the top line would grow 4.48% YoY. So it’s by far the slowest of the bunch. The issue lies more in that AVGO could lose out on sales to one of the longest standing customers it has had. What stops other chipmakers like AMD or Intel from also deciding to take the step at developing these chips? Not that much. Because this market (networking chips) and other similar ones like memory chips is playing catch up because NVDA went so far so fast with its GPU developments means that its open season. It’s ripe for disruption and AVGO has not established a MOAT just yet.

As I said before though, AVGO does have the first-move advantage. My concern is whether we’ll see an industry where everyone grows or if there will be just a few. Remember that AMD had a rough couple of years between 2022 and 2024 with slow top line growth compared to NVDA which completely dominated growing nearly 10x in revenue.

The Chip Story - Is There Any Validity Or Thesis Here?

I think the bull case for the chip story is quite interesting for AVGO. One argument I do think holds some validity is more philosophical than actual financial. NVDA is, as most people who follow it, strongly pushing for a closed garden where customers using one product use other services as well and these only interact with each other. This is the same philosophy that Apple (AAPL) has as well. AVGO is the opposite because of its use of Ethernet, an open-source standard. NVDA might make the better chip at the end of the day, but that means another part for hyperscalers is coming from a single company, creating an ever stronger hold on future developments.

One risk that held more weight a few weeks ago was that Alphabet (GOOGL), one of the largest customers to Broadcom, would be moving part of its chip making in house instead, shutting the door on AVGO and potentially suppliers like Marvell as well. But at the start of April Alphabet and AVGO signed a supply deal until 2031. This I think is definitely one of the reasons for the recent stock rally.

On the bearish side of things, which I think holds equal weight as the bullish, is the massive future market consolidation we’ll see here. It sort of mirrors what will happen in the memory market as well. In a recent article I wrote about Micron technology (MU) I talked about this. The similarity is strong, both markets are experiencing massive demand and there is an absolute shortage of these products. But once production catches up in the memory market or more competitors enter the networking industry, the market (and prices) will consolidate heavily. AVGO is competing against companies with deeper pockets and often more established positions in data centers already.

In just three years the CapEx spending for the Mag-7 has gone from below $50 billion in Q4 2022 to now over $120 billion in Q4 2025 with no signs of slowing down. Most of them hinted at an accelerated pace in 2026 with Amazon (AMZN) guiding for $200 billion in CapEx this year alone. This to me shows that we are in a “spend as much as possible to take as much as possible” era. These companies are already massively profitable with excellent balance sheets so the large spending on its own isn't really a concern I think. But because supply hasn't kept up with demand the revenues have exploded.

The inflection point I think will come this year and next year when a lot of the data centers will be built out and finished along with several large LLM companies set to IPO as well, like OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO). This will let investors see the actual economics of running these types of business models. But as more and more data centers are finished and these LLM companies fundamentally can’t really add more users.

OpenAI just a few days ago said it had 900 million weekly visitors. At this point it’s hard to see the growth being as big as it was a year or two ago. This user base needs to be served efficiently, and this is the inflection point. Data centers will be looking more at inference hardware, the things that make the models run the most efficient.

The company touts up to 90% lower token costs for certain types of AI models.

This year NVDA came out saying that their upcoming model Vera Rubin could manage to reduce the token count usage by 90%. The risk here for AVGO and in turn other networking chip makers is that if NVDA’s proprietary model can natively, meaning without additions, like a network chip reduce costs and data transfers, then what is the need for the chips AVGO makes? Why look to dual-source when you can kill two birds with one stone by just buying the GPU that NVDA makes. Sure, you're locked into one supplier but your CapEx just got a massive cut and leaving room to spend that elsewhere and consolidate market share.

The counter argument might be that AVGO is not just a hardware company, but also a massive software company. But the same growth is not there as in the first segment. YoY net revenues grew by 1.3% in Q1 FY2026. Operating margins did grow to 78.3%, up from 76.4%. This came from a decline across the board in all three cost lines. These revenues are incredibly sticky and predictable, but at 35% of the total company revenues and low single digit growth, it does not provide a strong counter argument against some of the risks I’ve mentioned. Besides, much of the growth has come from acquisitions here like VMWare. When AVGO now perhaps needs to spend more on R&D to ensure it has the best possible chip in its niche market, not a lot would be left for acquisitions to boost revenues here. I don’t think debt is a reliable path when there is $66 billion in total debt on the balance sheet.

The Full Picture

Broadcom is going to be a pass for me right now. I’m not buying the growth story which seems to suggest that it will have complete dominance in a market which is ripe for disruption in the near term if more established chip makers choose to enter. Besides this you have the risk that NVDA just completely shuts out AVGO by offering the same, or similar benefits with its upcoming models. That AVGO will see a rapid rise in its top line I have no argument against, it just seems right now that it might not be as sustainable or lasting as some think. For the moment I am more comfortable rating the stock a Hold as I begin coverage.

data.title: The Trade Desk: Current Valuation Appears Unusually Attractive
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Saga Partners Q1 2026 Investor Letter.

A steep decline in a stock price can reflect a real decline in business value, but it does not automatically mean something is broken or permanently impaired. Rather, it is a prompt to reexamine the thesis and ask whether the market's criticism is correct, exaggerated, or mistaken in light of the business's underlying economics and competitive dynamics.

The clearest current example in the Saga Portfolio is The Trade Desk (TTD), which is facing the greatest market skepticism and has experienced one of the largest drawdowns among our holdings. The Saga Portfolio first bought shares in 2017 based on the thesis that programmatic ad buying would grow, demand-side platforms (DSPs) would consolidate into a small number of scaled winners, and one of those winners would be an independent platform. More than eight years later, the main elements of that thesis have largely been borne out. The Trade Desk emerged as the leading scaled independent DSP, while revenue, earnings, and market value grew substantially.

In 2025, however, The Trade Desk's growth slowed. It is therefore reasonable for the market to question whether the slowdown is temporary or whether something more structural has changed, especially given how much expected growth had previously been built into the valuation. The market's main concerns have centered on increasing competition, particularly from Amazon's DSP, and the possibility that AI could weaken the position of companies like The Trade Desk.

While these risks should be taken seriously, neither the competitive threat from a walled-garden DSP nor the potential impact of AI is new, and I do not think recent developments materially change the long-term thesis. What has changed is that The Trade Desk's slower growth over the past year has led the market to attribute more of that slowdown to those risks rather than to near-term headwinds, and in turn to question the durability of the company's core competitive advantages more directly.

On competition, the value proposition of an independent platform remains the same: many advertisers still need a neutral platform to help them buy advertising across the open internet. That remains a structural advantage relative to a walled-garden DSP. Amazon may continue building a strong advertising business, but that does not mean it will be the best platform for buying advertising across the broader internet. More threatening to the long-term thesis would be the emergence of another scaled independent DSP with a similar value proposition and the ability to take meaningful share. So far, there is no evidence of that happening, and I think it would be very difficult for another independent DSP to emerge at meaningful scale.

On AI, the main concern is that it could make it easier for advertisers and publishers to work directly with one another through AI agents, reducing the need for a neutral platform and weakening certain intermediary functions. I do not think there is strong support for that concern, and the market may be too quick to view AI primarily as a threat rather than as a potential opportunity for a company like The Trade Desk.

What matters is not simply whether AI changes advertising, but how those changes affect the value of the platforms through which advertising decisions are made. The Trade Desk is best understood not simply as a software provider, but as a platform for advertising buyers across the open internet.

If AI improves how campaigns are planned, optimized, and measured, that could strengthen rather than weaken the value of a platform like The Trade Desk. Advertisers would still need a platform to coordinate budget allocation, data integration, measurement, and execution across the fragmented open internet. Greater automation could also make the platform easier to use and help it move further down market by serving smaller and mid-sized advertisers more effectively.

While the share price has declined sharply, the core value drivers of the business and the size of its long-term opportunity do not appear to have changed nearly as much. If The Trade Desk remains the leading independent DSP and AI proves more helpful than harmful, the long-term opportunity is still substantial, and the recent slowdown is more likely to prove temporary than structural. The company processed roughly $13 billion of ad spend over its platform last year, still only a fraction of the much larger open internet advertising market.

If that is right, the current valuation appears unusually attractive. With a market capitalization of roughly $10 billion, about $1.3 billion of net cash at year-end, and the ability to generate nearly $1 billion in free cash flow this year, the market seems to be pricing in a level of pessimism that is hard to reconcile with the company's long runway to grow earnings power.

One aspect of the current situation reminds me of the experience with Carvana (CVNA) in 2022. Not because the businesses are similar, but because in both cases market sentiment deteriorated far more than my long-term view of underlying value. Three years ago, selling a company widely viewed as a high-quality business like The Trade Desk to buy a deeply out-of-favor company like Carvana seemed absurd to many. Today, in some respects, market sentiment appears to have swung in the opposite direction. All the while, my long-term view of both companies has changed little despite the ups and downs in their share prices because their core drivers of value and long-term opportunity remain intact. Declining stock prices rarely come with reassuring headlines, and when those headlines push market sentiment far beyond what I would consider a conservative estimate of intrinsic value, they can create some of the most compelling mispricings.

Those reallocations were not attempts to trade around short-term price movements. They reflected changes in relative opportunity as prices moved far more than my estimate of underlying business value. In both cases, after I added to each holding, the shares fell further. The goal is not to identify the precise bottom, but to judge whether the current price offers an attractive long-term return relative to the alternatives available. For long-term owners of The Trade Desk, a depressed share price can be beneficial if it allows the company to repurchase more shares at attractive prices—just not our shares at current prices.

data.title: Kevin Warsh's Fed Confirmation Faces Tough Tests
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US and Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

Kevin Warsh's challenge - the President & market credibility

The Federal Reserve has entered its quiet period ahead of the 29 April FOMC meeting, but it will still be drawing headlines given Kevin Warsh's confirmation hearing to become Federal Reserve Chair is being held tomorrow. Once viewed as a hawk when he previously served as a Fed Governor (February 2006-March 2011), he has been nominated by President Trump, who has demanded much lower interest rates from current incumbent Jerome Powell.

Warsh will inevitably be questioned on how closely aligned he is with the President's thinking. We imagine he will indicate a belief that, over time, lower interest rates make sense. However, preserving market credibility is of paramount importance, and there must be justification.

The Iran conflict-induced jump in motor fuel costs creates near-term challenges in an environment where both core and headline consumer price inflation have exceeded the 2% target throughout the past five years. He will be wary of coming across as too dovish. That could heighten market fears of potential unanchored market inflation expectations, prompting higher long-term borrowing costs.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has emphasized that, in his view, the most important metric is the 10Y Treasury yield given its benchmark role and the impact it has on mortgage rates and corporate borrowing costs. He appears to be onboard with a delay to rate cuts, based on recent media comments, but the President is probably not going to be so keen. Nonetheless, if there is a swift conclusion to the conflict and fuel prices drop sharply, Warsh will strongly suggest there is the possibility of his support for rate cuts later this year.

Structurally speaking, Kevin Warsh will emphasize the opportunity for lower borrowing costs over the medium to longer term. He clearly buys into the idea that tech/AI investment will boost productivity and allow the US to grow faster without generating inflation. To be fair, there appears to be support for this view within the broader Fed, given that within the March forecast update, officials revised higher their long-run GDP forecast to 2% from 1.8%, without altering the long-term inflation prediction. The minutes suggested that "several participants… expected higher productivity growth, associated with technological or deregulatory developments, to put downward pressure on inflation."

The Fed’s balance sheet

In addition to the productivity narrative, Kevin Warsh has another theory. Every US$1tn of Fed bond holdings equates to 50bp on the policy rate (approximately). In consequence, balance sheet contraction, through bond roll-offs or outright selling, facilitates rate cuts. This is convenient, as Warsh also believes that the Fed continues to own too many bonds. The build of bond buying was a consequence of past crises, and now that they are no more, much of the bonds should be offloaded. He’s not been specific about sizes but said that they should be significantly reduced. And he’s identified mortgage-backed securities (MBS) as a particular eyesore that should be liquidated. And finally, he’s advocated mostly a passive run-off, cognizant of market de-stabilization risks.

Consider two scenarios. One is a reduction in the balance sheet right back to pre-Global Financial Crisis levels (5.5% of GDP). That would require some US$4.5tn in bond liquidations. That’s unlikely, but we do develop that narrative here. More probable is a targeted sale of the US$2tn of MBS holdings. This is currently rolling off at a slow trickle, as it's primarily long in duration. But it could be subject to outright sales. It could be softened by extra T-bills buying to prevent repo market disruption.

The issue here, however, is the impact on bank reserves. Bank reserves sit on the Fed’s balance sheet and are remunerated by the rate the Fed pays on reserves. They are there as an echo of the prior bond buying undertaken by the Fed (effectively the other side of the Fed’s balance sheet). In 2019 and again through the second half of 2025, we saw repo market tightness occur as bank reserves fell to critical levels. For no particular reason, circa 10% of GDP is a level below which tightness has tended to kick in.

Kevin Warsh has suggested identification of a target for bank reserves and keeping to that. He’s not been specific as to what that level should be. The unwritten level today is US$3tn. That’s sitting alongside Fed bond holdings of US$6.3tn. Simplistically, if the Fed’s balance sheet was shrunk by US$2tn, bank reserves could be pressured down to around US$2-2.5tn. In today’s setup, that would cause repo issues.

The simple solution would be to offset MBS sales with T-bills buying. Not technically QE (as bills buying is short in maturity and, in a sense, akin to open market operations), but would still leave the balance sheet bloated with government securities. Warsh needs a plan to deal with this. We’ll be listening for more as he testifies, albeit perhaps a bit deep in the weeds for a Senate hearing. It is really important, though. Apart from repo issues, MBS selling with a view to cutting rates points to a steeper curve from both ends.

Chair Powell could continue beyond 15 May

Whether Kevin Warsh will be approved quickly is another matter given the Department of Justice's probe into Fed construction work overspending. The Senate Banking Committee consists of 13 Republicans and 11 Democrats, but Republican Thom Tillis has vowed to block the confirmation until the "vindictive prosecution" of Chair Powell is ended. Until Thom Tillis is satisfied, it will be a 12-12 vote, and it can't proceed to a formal Senate vote.

Jerome Powell’s term as Fed Chair notionally ends on 15 May, but until Warsh is approved, Powell will stay in the role, with his term as Governor scheduled to continue until 31 January 2028. As such, tensions between the Fed and the President are likely to remain elevated, but with strong rules of governance, we think the Fed’s independence is not in doubt.

data.title: Don't Confuse Market Confidence With Complacency
data.description:
By Geoff Bysshe

You must have heard, but should you have seen this coming?

The Nasdaq 100 (NDX) closed on Friday with its longest winning streak since 2013.

As you can see in the chart below (created on Thursday) by Ryan Detric from the Carson Group, 13 days is rare. Two more would be the longest since 1990!

Later, we’ll show that this strength hasn’t always meant smooth sailing ahead, but it is generally bullish, nonetheless.

More importantly, could you have anticipated the market’s rally, and what should you do now?

The Market Was Confident, Not Complacent

Since the beginning of March, when the war in the Middle East began raging, oil and critical commodity prices have climbed at record rates, global trade has been increasingly disrupted, and long-standing geopolitical relationships have been fracturing.

At the same time, analysts have been raising earnings estimates, AI capital investment has been booming, and new AI capabilities with productive and disruptive implications have been announced almost daily.

All the while, the stock market discounts expectations of future corporate earnings growth, and its answer is reflected in the price action.

As the market sold off, the bears described the slow pace of correction as complacency. The bulls described it as confidence.

A Confident Bull Market Doesn’t Wait For The Fundamentals To Be Obvious

A confident bull market absorbs negative news; a complacent bull market gets caught off guard and freaks out.

Most weeks, this article focuses on what the market’s message is suggesting about the direction of the trend moving forward.

The stock market’s reaction to the war was like a game of chicken between the bulls and bears, with the rising prices of critical commodities like oil, fertilizer, and more being the pain that the bears were betting would break the bulls, while both sides surmised that oil prices would reverse if the war ended without too much long-term damage to oil-producing infrastructure and global supply chains.

Looking back at the titles of our recent articles and then pulling the message together with the charts below provides a good summary of how the bulls won the game of chicken over the impact of the war.

The next game won’t be over the war, but it will involve similar analysis.

The Market’s Messages Leading Up to the 13-day Explosive Rally

The weekend the war began, March 1, Market Outlook’s focus was:

“Earnings Season Ends On A Down Note; Next Up: Rates, Risk, And Rotation”

We wrote...

Last week, earnings season “ended” with a disappointing reaction to the much-anticipated Nvidia earnings.

And concluded...

Historical safe havens (the U.S. dollar, Treasury Bonds, gold, etc.) and scarcity-driven resources (oil) are likely to be bid up initially. Stocks are a tougher call in the short term, but a longer-term perspective suggests the war is less of a concern than the primary drivers of stock prices - consumer spending and corporate profits.

A week into the war, March 8th Market Outlook’s theme was:

“Was Last Week The Tipping Point For Stocks?”

We asked...

Last week was filled with more than a few small bearish events, but did they create a tipping point for the bull market?

We concluded...

The positive news is that last week, the bullish narrative enabled the bulls to be optimistic that the bullish trends in earnings expectations (we discussed this last week) and a stable state of GDP growth would enable the market to see through a temporary supply shock created by a “quick war” with Iran.

On March 15, with oil prices up about 50% since the start of the war, Market Outlook’s theme was:

“Time Is Running Out”

We wrote...

Bull markets and strong economies are killed by “shocks.”

The difference between a problem and a shock can be measured in time and magnitude. I don’t think many investors would debate that the magnitude of the war with Iran qualifies as a shock. Now the question is, “How big will the shock be?” For the bulls looking for a resumption of the bull market,…

Time is running out.

We concluded...

In short, the data does not point to an economic collapse, but it suggests the economy may be losing momentum faster than previously thought. If growth fails to rebound as expected, the economy could shift from being a tailwind for markets to becoming a potential headwind for investors.

On March 29th, with The SPY and QQQ closing below their 200-day moving average, Market Outlook’s theme was:

“The Market’s Big Problem Is Persistence. Why and When To Buy GLW, CIEN and…”

We asked...

How Will This Market Bottom?

There are three common ways market corrections and bear markets bottom out.

  1. The first is quick and violent capitulation.
  2. The second is a process of sector rotation, and
  3. The third is through a long depression of investor sentiment, combined with compressed price action.

We concluded...

The investors and the media are looking out for capitulation (number 1), but the market is saying it prefers rotation right now.

In other words, while the market is trading like a bear market and the media’s reports often sound dire, the market is not selling everything in a rush of capitulation.

If you’re only looking at the S&P 500 or NASDAQ 100, it may feel like capitulation is underway, but the broader market tells a different story.

After what felt like the longest March ever, On April 5th, Market Outlook was able to report:

“Watch Out - Last Week Was Different; Space Stock Takes Off, and Energy Stocks Fall”

We highlighted...

With the war taking center stage, it was easy to overlook the economic data that was surprisingly optimistic if you’re focused on growth, but less so if you’re worried about inflation. Was this what disrupted the stock/oil correlation?

We concluded...

Last week was chock full of “firsts” and “changes” that may turn “green shoots” into the next major market narrative.

The break in correlations with oil made last week’s price action very different and potentially bullish. The size of the move was also impressive.

The next “first” we’ll be looking for in our trading room will be a weekly close significantly over a prior week’s high. That hasn’t happened in the SPY or QQQ in 2026. That’s a simple first sign of a durable reversal.

With the war in focus, it was easy to overlook the economic data that was remarkably optimistic if you’re focused on growth, but less so if you’re concerned about inflation.

With the stocks already rallying before the ceasefire, the question on investors' minds was, “bounce” or “bottom”? Our answer in Market Outlook last week was:

“War, Inflation - No Problem; Next Challenge - Earnings”

We wrote:

The ceasefire gave the stock market the “move past the war” catalyst it had spent the prior week preparing for. The next hurdle will be the wave of bank earnings this week.

We concluded:

In last week’s Market Outlook, we highlighted the market rotation that was forming a bottom, and economic news that could support a more bullish market narrative.

This was supported by the fact that the bond market had also stopped going down in the face of higher crude prices and other news that would normally put pressure on rates to move higher.

The market was ready to rally before the ceasefire news so the welcome catalyst created a strong rally that held up and followed through in the indexes and several key sectors.

The war’s market bottom is in. Now it’s up to earnings to keep the bull market intact. With earnings taking center stage, the economy, interest rates, and inflation will be the supporting cast of characters.

Here’s How The Market Narratives Above Played Out In The Charts

Even before the new year starts, I start talking about which indexes, sectors and stocks will likely be good opportunities based on the trading tactic we call Calendar Ranges. The big ones occur in January and July, and they are based on 10 days - not the whole month!

These ranges are illustrated on each of the markets in the chart below. The simplest application is to be bullish above the Jan. Calendar Range (CR) High (green) and bearish below the CR Low (red). Additionally, you look for confirmation between instruments, as you’ll read below the chart.

The weekly narratives described above were also supported by and discussed in Market Outlook articles, but the progression in 2026 from neutral to slightly bearish, and finally to bullish for stocks, can be described like this.

  1. 2026 began with a neutral bias, as stocks did not break out of their January highs.
  2. In February, big tech dragged down SPY and QQQ as the equal-weight S&P (and IWM not shown here) broke out to the upside, following the lower rates as bonds (TLT) broke out higher.
  3. When we attacked Iran in the beginning of March, TLT and IWM topped out and fell back into their January ranges. QQQ and SPY (not shown) held their already weak position relative to their January range low. Oil (USO), which was already in a bullish position over its January CR High, jumped and continued higher.

    As the war escalated throughout the month of March, oil remained high. Bonds traded lower consistently, as the duration of the war is likely to be related to the future duration of its inflationary effects.

    Stocks fell reluctantly, and sector rotation created a slow but highly correlated correction between stocks, bonds, and oil. Higher oil prices pushed bonds down, which pushed stocks down.
  4. In late March, oil pulled back from its highs, and contrary to recent correlation, bonds fell too, and so did stocks. Within a few days, oil resumed its uptrend and broke out to new highs, but the oil-stock correlation was broken. Stocks were rallying too!
  5. On the new move up in oil, bonds did not move to new lows, and stocks took notice. Stocks and bonds were signaling that they had priced in oil at the elevated levels.

    Stocks adapt to new interest rate levels quickly. It’s quickly changing interest rates that create the problem. If rates were done going down, then stocks were ready to go up.

    As a result, the rally in stocks began.

You Don’t Need To Be A Macro Market Analyst (just read the charts)

While this inter-market relationships dance was playing out, you didn’t need to look any further than the market index charts (SPY, QQQ, IWM, and DIA or RSP), to see the evidence that the bottom of the correction was near.

Below, you can see the same story from another perspective.

  1. The QQQ actually peaked in late October 2025. Last week was the first time it exceeded that high. This high correlated with a high in TLT that was roughly in line with the 2025 high in TLT.
  2. The lack of a new high in QQQ is relevant when you consider that the SPY, IWM, DIA (not shown), and RSP all made new all-time highs in Jan 2026. Leadership had rotated away from QQQ and broadened out as evidenced by RSP breaking its Jan CR High.
  3. The previous leader, QQQ, broke its Jan. CR Low and its 50-day MA. This is a sign of weakness.
  4. The SPY broke its Jan. CR Low and its 50-day MA. This is another sign of weakness, but the correction is still less than -10%, above its 200-day MA, and the broader market represented by IWM and RSP had not broken their Calendar Range Low.
  5. The “headfake” at the 200-day! A capitulation low is much more dramatic or violent than the pattern we saw on the last day of March, which also concluded the first quarter.The “headfake” pattern low is a big range down day that closes near the low of the range, followed by an open that is near or over the prior down day's high. Here's the SPY's example, and every stock index had this pattern!

    • This pattern says, “buy my breakout, and set your stop below my low or the low of the correction”.

      When it occurs at the 200-day MA for the IWM and RSP, you can’t ignore it.

      Furthermore, it created a reversal at their January Calendar Range lows and 200-day MA.

      • Every seller from the day before feels duped.
      • If you’re short, you’re uncomfortable.
      • If it happens in an area that represented a break of major support (as was the case at the 200 DMA of the IWM and RSP), the savvy active investor knows the bears are trapped, and there’s a well-defined risk in getting long.
  6. The additional nuance here is that the two broadest indexes had this pattern at the 200-day MA, and the two weakest indexes (SPY and QQQ) had the same bullish pattern.The stock market was saying that the correction low was defined on this day so long as the next day didn’t completely reverse it.

All this happened days before the ceasefire gap higher.

The ceasefire would almost certainly create a positive reaction, but the fact that the market was already clearly looking beyond (or anticipating) the ceasefire meant the reaction to the ceasefire would continue.

What’s Next

Late Friday, Iran announced that the Strait of Hormuz was closed.This should be bearish, as it’s potentially a break in the ceasefire, but on Sunday morning, the administration announced that talks with Iran would be held again in Pakistan on Monday.

Right on schedule. Have you noticed the administration’s ability to announce news that the market should interpret positively on Sundays?

One of Iran’s tactics is to keep oil prices high to hurt the administration and the US consumer.

What we’ve said above demonstrates that higher oil prices are harmful, and so are expectations of future higher prices!

As a result, part of the war effort is the administration's jawboning of the markets to expect and encourage lower oil prices and higher stock prices.

Don’t Let The News Distract From What the Market Is Telling You

Listen to the news and media, but let the market’s interpretation or “message” that comes from it drive your active trading decisions.

The war will create speed bumps, but currently, until it creates fears of a recession, it’s a nuisance, not a reason to expect a major correction unless the market drops back below the 200-day MA. This would suggest there is a problem you need to focus on.

The 13-Day Streak

Above, we suggested that the 13-day streak is not an "all clear" signal.

As you can see from the table below provided by the Carson Group, I could have focused on “100% bullish” over the next 12 months, but 1-, 3-, and 6-month returns are much less impressive.

The encouraging stat from the table above is that if the market is higher 3 months later, it tends to be higher 6 and 12 months later.

Earnings Will Be The Driver And The Banks Delivered

As we said in last week’s Market Outlook, earnings season is likely to be the biggest driver of the trend over the coming weeks. Last week, many of the major banks reported.

As you can see from the table below, all of the banks listed beat EPS estimates, and most beat their revenue expectations.

Most importantly, the market’s reaction was mostly bullish.

Off To A Good Start

Earnings season is off to a good start.

For the first time this year, all the major stock indexes are above their January CR High, except the Dow (DIA), which stopped at its CR High.

This broad multi-index breakout is bullish. If or until the markets close below their January calendar range highs, the bull market is headed higher.

Best wishes for your trading.

Big View Summary

By Keith Schneider

Every week, we review the big picture of the market's technical condition as seen through the lens of our Big View data charts.

The bullets provide a quick summary organized by conditions we see as being risk-on, risk-off, or neutral.

Summary: Markets surged into new highs with broad participation, strong accumulation, falling volatility, and leadership from growth, confirming a powerful risk-on environment supported by both domestic and global strength.

However, short-term internals are becoming stretched with overbought readings and key assets like commodities, gold, and rates at potential inflection points, suggesting the rally may face near-term consolidation or pauses.

Risk-On

  • Markets put in an explosive week, with 3 of the four indexes making new all-time highs. On price, all of the markets are hugging the upper Bollinger bands, though they are not overbought on real motion. (+)
  • Volume patterns are confirming the bullish move with 5+ accumulation days in all the indexes and only one distribution day between them all. (+)
  • Sectors were nearly unanimously positive, with the exception of energy because of the sell-off in crude and the safety play of utilities. (+)
  • Volatility moved lower, with energy prices confirming bullish price action. (+)
  • The color charts (moving average of stocks above key moving averages) on a short-term and intermediate basis are 100% bullish and bullish to neutral on longer-term readings. (+)
  • Risk gauges remain quite strong with 4 of the 5 ratios risk-on, with the wood/lumbar ratio being the only hold-out. (+)
  • Volatility continued to come off this week and is now in a distribution phase. (+)
  • The value vs. growth relationship has growth resuming leadership. While both are pushing new recent highs, neither has taken out their all-time highs. (+)
  • The Modern Family continued its recent strength, with all of the members back in bullish phases. (+)
  • Foreign markets were up sharply and are in bullish phases in both emerging and developed markets, with emerging markets testing new all-time highs. Both markets look like they could put in a double top at these levels. (+)
  • Bitcoin continued its nascent trend and put in its highest levels since early February. (+)
  • Seasonal trends remain strong for April and the next few months. (+)

Neutral

  • Market internals all put in new highs this week, continuing the recent trend. The McClellan Oscillator did reach overbought levels. (=)
  • The 52-week new high new low ratio surged. It is at an extreme overbought levels on the short term but otherwise looks very positive. (=)
  • Soft commodities remained in their recent trading range from the last month. Copper looks like it has some overhead resistance. (=)
  • Gold could be at an interesting inflection point, coming right back up to its 50-Day Moving Average, and it will be interesting to see which way it inflects. (=)
  • Short-term rates eased a little this week but are still locked into bearish phases and in the midst of a trading range. (=)

Actionable Trading Plan (Based on Current Regime: Strong Risk-On, Short-Term Stretched)

1. Core Positioning (Stay Invested, Lean Long)

  • Maintain a high net long exposure - this is not an environment to fade strength.
  • Prioritize leadership: growth, semis, high-beta names, and areas making new highs (including selective EM and crypto proxies).
  • Avoid laggards (energy, soft commodities) unless they show clear relative strength reversals.

2. Add-on Strategy (Buy Strength, Not Weakness)

  • Add to positions on continuation moves (breakouts or strong follow-through days), not dips.
  • Scale in rather than all-in - momentum is strong, but short-term conditions are stretched.
  • Focus on names/sectors confirming with volume (accumulation trends matter here).

3. Risk Management (Respect Extension Without Overreacting)

  • Tighten stops modestly on extended positions - this is where sharp, fast pullbacks can occur.
  • If internals deteriorate (e.g., breadth weakens, distribution days cluster), reduce exposure incrementally - not all at once.
  • Avoid initiating large new positions if multiple overbought signals cluster simultaneously.

4. Rotation Awareness (Watch the Inflection Points)

  • Monitor gold, copper, and rates closely - any decisive move could signal cross-asset rotation.
  • Be alert for early signs of leadership change (e.g., value reasserting, defensive sectors catching bids).
  • If volatility begins expanding from low levels, shift from aggressive adds to capital preservation.

5. Tactical Hedge / Cash Use

  • Keep some dry powder (~10-20%) to take advantage of any fast pullbacks.
  • No need for heavy hedging yet - volatility is declining - but be ready to act quickly if regime shifts.

6. Time Horizon Framing

    • Short term (days-weeks): Expect chop or consolidation due to overbought conditions.
    • Intermediate term (weeks-months): Trend remains bullish unless internals materially break.

Bottom Line

Stay long and participate in the trend, but shift from aggressive accumulation to disciplined scaling and tighter risk control as the market gets extended.

data.description:
Q1 2026 Results

During Q1 2026, the Saga Portfolio declined 23.0% net of fees, compared to a 4.3% decline in the S&P 500 Index, including dividends.
