(2) S&P 500 performance includes dividends. Please see disclaimer at the end of this letter regarding comparison to indices.

I have shifted toward writing annual letters because, over a three- or six-month period, there is often little genuinely new to say about either how the Saga Portfolio is managed or the underlying businesses we own. Frequent updates also risk repeating the same core ideas and drawing unnecessary attention to short-term market fluctuations. Even so, the first quarter brought the first material drawdown in the Saga Portfolio in three years, and I thought it was a good opportunity to address it directly and explain more clearly how I think about drawdowns.

The quarter brought renewed focus to headline risks, including concerns around AI disruption and geopolitical tensions involving Iran. Any development that could materially affect the long-term value of a business deserves serious attention. That does not mean the goal is to guess what stock prices will do in the short term, but to assess whether new information changes a company's intrinsic value. In many cases, that direct impact on a company's core value drivers and long-term prospects is smaller than short-term share price movements might suggest.

The Trade Desk provides a useful example of how I think through those issues in practice, and I will return to it later in the letter. Its stock price drawdown reflects not only the broader market response to recent headline risks, but also company-specific concerns about the business and its outlook. Before turning to the Saga Portfolio more specifically, however, I think it is helpful to step back and revisit a few broader ideas about compounding, volatility, and what the Saga Portfolio is trying to do.

The Power of Compounding

For most of human history, there was little opportunity to compound wealth in any meaningful way. Economic growth was nearly nonexistent, surplus capital was scarce, and living standards improved little across generations.

What changed was not simply that people became smarter or worked harder. It was that some societies developed traditions that were more conducive to progress. A culture of criticism emerged, making it easier to question assumptions, challenge authority, correct mistakes, and search for better explanations. As error-correction improved, knowledge grew more quickly. The Enlightenment, and later the Industrial Revolution, turned that knowledge into practical improvements in production, transportation, communication, and health. Living standards rose and, for the first time in history, wealth could compound on a broad scale.

Public equity markets eventually made it possible for ordinary savers to own shares in productive businesses and thereby participate in the long-term growth of earnings power across the economy. Over the last century, the S&P 500 (SP500) has generated returns of roughly 10% annually. Those returns did not come from stocks as abstract symbols on a screen. They came from businesses solving problems, serving customers, reinvesting capital, and growing their earnings power over time.

The math of compounding is simple: , where is the number of years. Each variable matters, but time is what gives compounding its exponential power. Given enough time, even a relatively modest rate of return can turn a small initial sum into something very large. The longer compounding continues, the more the results become concentrated in the later years.

Benjamin Franklin understood compounding well enough to leave long-dated bequests to Boston and Philadelphia in his will, structured to earn interest over generations. But even Franklin might have been surprised by what public equity ownership eventually made possible. His £1,000 gift in 1790 was roughly equivalent to $4,400 at the time. At an 8% annual return, that initial sum would have grown to more than $300 billion by 2025. Few investors have a 200-year horizon, but the example illustrates how dramatic the effects of compounding can become over time.

That is why Warren Buffett made over 99% of his wealth after what most people would consider retirement age. That is not some strange exception. It is simply what happens when capital compounds for long enough.

The Challenge of Compounding in Real Time

A 10% annual return over a century is a remarkable outcome, but in real time there are major obstacles that stand between investors and the benefits of compounding.

One is that there is no law that says progress must occur continuously or smoothly. Future returns depend on businesses continuing to solve problems, create value, and grow their earnings power. Because future returns are uncertain, investors can never know for sure whether a new risk is temporary or a sign that something more fundamental has changed. That uncertainty can tempt even long-term investors to prefer the apparent safety of cash or bonds, even at the cost of giving up much of the benefit of owning productive assets.

Even when businesses are improving over time, the experience of owning stocks is rarely smooth. Share prices move not only in response to business results, but also to changing expectations, criticism, uncertainty, and the continual discovery of new problems and possibilities. Drawdowns and reassessments are not interruptions to the process of compounding. They are part of what it looks like in real time.

The last decade is a good example. The S&P 500 returned an exceptional 14% annually over that period, yet it also experienced frequent drawdowns, including several declines of 20% or more. During the onset of the COVID pandemic, the S&P 500 fell by more than 35% in a matter of weeks despite being a highly diversified index.

S&P 500 Index drawdowns over the past decade:

# Start End Decline Headline Risk
1 Nov 2015 Feb 2016 -14.5% Global growth fears, credit stress
2 Jan 2018 Feb 2018 -11.8% Inflation, rising interest rates
3 Sep 2018 Dec 2018 -20.2% Fed tightening, trade war
4 Feb 2020 Mar 2020 -35.4% COVID, global shutdown
5 Sep 2020 Sep 2020 -10.6% Election uncertainty, COVID resurgence
6 Jan 2022 Jun 2022 -24.5% Inflation, Fed tightening, Russia/Ukraine
7 Aug 2022 Oct 2022 -19.3% Fed tightening, recession fears
8 Jul 2023 Oct 2023 -10.9% Higher-for-longer interest rates
9 Feb 2025 Apr 2025 -21.3% Tariffs, trade war
10 Jan 2026 Mar 2026 -9.8% AI disruption, Iran conflict

Source: Saga Partners, FactSet

That volatility can lead investors to believe they can improve results by timing the market in an attempt to avoid drawdowns. Yet short-term stock price movements cannot be predicted reliably for a fundamental reason. Prices reflect not only current facts, but future knowledge and future human responses that do not yet exist and therefore cannot be known in advance. That does not mean share prices are necessarily accurate. It means outperformance is unlikely to come from consistently predicting short-term market moves.

At times, general sentiment may clearly swing to an extreme of fear or greed, and those moments can create unusually favorable opportunities. Even then, however, the core decision is the same: not to anticipate short-term shifts in sentiment for their own sake, but to judge whether prices imply better long-term returns than the alternatives available. The only practical way I know to navigate this reality is to invest where the gap between price and value appears widest, based on the best explanations available, and to do so with the expectation that the capital will remain invested for at least the next decade.

Why Active Management Can Make Sense

A low-cost index fund is a sensible default option. It gives investors ownership of a broad cross-section of productive businesses while helping them avoid many of the errors that plague active management.

That matters because wealth creation in markets is highly uneven. A small number of companies account for a very large share of long-term wealth creation, while most stocks underperform the market and many produce disappointing or even negative lifetime returns. Indexing gives investors ownership of the next Apple, Amazon, or Google before the market fully reflects the value those businesses will ultimately create, even though it also means owning many more of the businesses that decline or disappear.

This skewed outcome is not surprising in a knowledge-creating economy. Most companies do not discover fundamentally better ways of serving customers, and even those that once did often stagnate as new competitors

emerge with better ideas. The few that continue finding better ways to solve important problems can create extraordinary value, and the economic rewards can be far larger than those of the average firm.

The purpose of the Saga Portfolio is to invest in businesses that appear unusually well positioned to create substantial long-term value, but whose share prices still do not fully reflect that future earnings power. The goal is not to depend on macroeconomic forecasts, avoid drawdowns, or use leverage to chase extraordinary returns. It is to be selective and invest where the gap between price and value appears widest.

If that analysis is directionally correct, the portfolio should outperform not by predicting short-term market movements, but by owning more of the businesses that drive an outsized share of long-term wealth creation. Active management can make sense for investors willing to put in the time and effort required to identify better opportunities, understand them thoroughly, size them appropriately, and hold them through greater volatility. Given the power of compounding, even a modest edge can produce a materially different long-term outcome over time.

Evaluating Stock Declines: The Trade Desk (TTD)

A steep decline in a stock price can reflect a real decline in business value, but it does not automatically mean something is broken or permanently impaired. Rather, it is a prompt to reexamine the thesis and ask whether the market's criticism is correct, exaggerated, or mistaken in light of the business's underlying economics and competitive dynamics.

The clearest current example in the Saga Portfolio is The Trade Desk, which is facing the greatest market skepticism and has experienced one of the largest drawdowns among our holdings. The Saga Portfolio first bought shares in 2017 based on the thesis that programmatic ad buying would grow, demand-side platforms (DSPs) would consolidate into a small number of scaled winners, and one of those winners would be an independent platform. More than eight years later, the main elements of that thesis have largely been borne out. The Trade Desk emerged as the leading scaled independent DSP, while revenue, earnings, and market value grew substantially.

In 2025, however, The Trade Desk's growth slowed. It is therefore reasonable for the market to question whether the slowdown is temporary or whether something more structural has changed, especially given how much expected growth had previously been built into the valuation. The market's main concerns have centered on increasing competition, particularly from Amazon's DSP, and the possibility that AI could weaken the position of companies like The Trade Desk.

While these risks should be taken seriously, neither the competitive threat from a walled-garden DSP nor the potential impact of AI is new, and I do not think recent developments materially change the long-term thesis. What has changed is that The Trade Desk's slower growth over the past year has led the market to attribute more of that slowdown to those risks rather than to near-term headwinds, and in turn to question the durability of the company's core competitive advantages more directly.

On competition, the value proposition of an independent platform remains the same: many advertisers still need a neutral platform to help them buy advertising across the open internet. That remains a structural advantage relative to a walled-garden DSP. Amazon may continue building a strong advertising business, but that does not mean it will be the best platform for buying advertising across the broader internet. More threatening to the long-term thesis would be the emergence of another scaled independent DSP with a similar value proposition and the ability to take meaningful share. So far, there is no evidence of that happening, and I think it would be very difficult for another independent DSP to emerge at meaningful scale.

On AI, the main concern is that it could make it easier for advertisers and publishers to work directly with one another through AI agents, reducing the need for a neutral platform and weakening certain intermediary functions. I do not think there is strong support for that concern, and the market may be too quick to view AI primarily as a threat rather than as a potential opportunity for a company like The Trade Desk.

What matters is not simply whether AI changes advertising, but how those changes affect the value of the platforms through which advertising decisions are made. The Trade Desk is best understood not simply as a software provider, but as a platform for advertising buyers across the open internet.

If AI improves how campaigns are planned, optimized, and measured, that could strengthen rather than weaken the value of a platform like The Trade Desk. Advertisers would still need a platform to coordinate budget allocation, data integration, measurement, and execution across the fragmented open internet. Greater automation could also make the platform easier to use and help it move further down market by serving smaller and mid-sized advertisers more effectively.

While the share price has declined sharply, the core value drivers of the business and the size of its long-term opportunity do not appear to have changed nearly as much. If The Trade Desk remains the leading independent DSP and AI proves more helpful than harmful, the long-term opportunity is still substantial, and the recent slowdown is more likely to prove temporary than structural. The company processed roughly $13 billion of ad spend over its platform last year, still only a fraction of the much larger open internet advertising market.

If that is right, the current valuation appears unusually attractive. With a market capitalization of roughly $10 billion, about $1.3 billion of net cash at year-end, and the ability to generate nearly $1 billion in free cash flow this year, the market seems to be pricing in a level of pessimism that is hard to reconcile with the company's long runway to grow earnings power.

One aspect of the current situation reminds me of the experience with Carvana (CVNA) in 2022. Not because the businesses are similar, but because in both cases market sentiment deteriorated far more than my long-term view of underlying value. Three years ago, selling a company widely viewed as a high-quality business like The Trade Desk to buy a deeply out-of-favor company like Carvana seemed absurd to many. Today, in some respects, market sentiment appears to have swung in the opposite direction. All the while, my long-term view of both companies has changed little despite the ups and downs in their share prices because their core drivers of value and long-term opportunity remain intact. Declining stock prices rarely come with reassuring headlines, and when those headlines push market sentiment far beyond what I would consider a conservative estimate of intrinsic value, they can create some of the most compelling mispricings.

Those reallocations were not attempts to trade around short-term price movements. They reflected changes in relative opportunity as prices moved far more than my estimate of underlying business value. In both cases, after I added to each holding, the shares fell further. The goal is not to identify the precise bottom, but to judge whether the current price offers an attractive long-term return relative to the alternatives available. For long-term owners of The Trade Desk, a depressed share price can be beneficial if it allows the company to repurchase more shares at attractive prices—just not our shares at current prices.

Conclusion

Everything seems obvious in hindsight. In real time, investing looks very different: knowledge is incomplete, conditions change, and prices often move far more than business value. That is what makes investing difficult, but it is also what creates the possibility of mispricing. The goal is not to know in advance exactly what stock prices will do, but to build the best explanations we can, compare them with the prices being offered, and act accordingly. Nothing about short-term market fluctuations changes that process.

For much of the last three years, several of our holdings have appeared unusually attractive to me, which is one reason there have been so few new companies added to the portfolio. It has been difficult to find other opportunities that seem clearly better. I have often wanted to add more to a position, limited only by my reluctance to sell anything else.

When I look at our companies today, I see businesses that continue to strengthen their competitive positions and have long runways to grow their earnings power, even if that growth does not always occur in a straight line. Their shares also trade meaningfully below what I think they will ultimately be worth. I do not know what their share prices will do over the next quarter or year. But based on the best explanations I have today, I expect the companies we own at current prices to compound our capital at attractive rates over the long term.

I remain grateful for your trust and partnership as we continue working to compound capital over time. Please feel free to reach out if you have any questions or would like to connect.

Sincerely,

Joe Frankenfield

data.title: HCA Healthcare's Health Check-Up Reveals Opportunity As Earnings Near
data.description:
In the time that I have been writing about HCA Healthcare, Inc. (HCA), things have gone quite well for the company. The last article that I wrote about the business was published in April of last year. I acknowledged that there were certain regulatory uncertainties that could negatively affect the business. But even so, I maintained that the stock was attractively priced and that there was a real opportunity for continued growth. Since reaffirming it as a ‘buy’ candidate in that article, the stock has risen 53.4%. That's well above the 36.8% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But that's nothing. Since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ back in August of 2021, the stock has jumped 104.1%. That dwarfs the 61.7% increase that the S&P 500 experienced over the same window of time.

Fundamentally speaking, the business is doing a fine job. Revenue, profits, and cash flows are all rising. The number of licensed beds that it has increased. Occupancy rates have risen. And the revenue that it is getting from each equivalent admission continues to grow as the number of visits to its locations rises. When you add all of this together and combine it with the fact that shares still look cheap on an absolute basis, though they are perhaps closer to being fairly valued compared to other similar firms, I think that maintaining it as a very soft ‘buy’ makes sense here.

This does not mean that my opinion can't or won't change as new data justifies it. It just so happens that, before the market opens on April 24th, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. As we approach that date, analysts believe that revenue and earnings will both increase. But of course, investors should always be diligent. The picture can always change, sometimes rapidly. So it would be wise to pay attention to what news does come out. Unless something serious happens, however, or unless the stock price rises nicely in response to its earnings release, I suspect that keeping it as a soft ‘buy’ will make sense.

HCA Healthcare Is Looking Healthy

In the event that you have never looked at HCA Healthcare before, it is helpful to know exactly what the business is and what it does. Management describes the company as one of the leading healthcare services firms in the US. It operates 190 hospitals, with 169 of those being general acute care hospitals. Seven are behavioral hospitals. And four are rehabilitation hospitals. The firm also operates 121 freestanding ambulatory surgery centers and 31 freestanding endoscopy centers. All of these assets are spread across 19 states, plus England of all places. With a footprint this large, it's no wonder that the company has such a significant market capitalization. As of this writing, it's worth $109.1 billion. That is absolutely massive.

Over the last three years now, management has achieved some attractive growth. From 2023 through 2025, revenue jumped from $65 billion to $75.6 billion. This rise in revenue is the result of multiple factors. First and foremost, over the last three years, the number of licensed beds that the company has grown from 49,588 to 50,436. On top of this, the firm saw a small increase in occupancy from 72.3% to 73.1%. There have been some other fascinating indicators as well. Take, if you will, the number of emergency room visits that the business has exhibited. Back in 2023, these came in at 9.34 million. But by 2025, the number had risen to 9.95 million. Inpatient surgery cases grew during this time also, climbing from 528,845 to 545,405. This is not to say that every category saw growth. The number of outpatient surgery cases, for instance, actually contracted from 1.04 million to 1.02 million. But on the whole, this did not stop equivalent admissions from expanding from 3.79 million to 4.11 million.

I do think it's worth also pointing out that, in addition to seeing a rise in the number of admissions, the company has also benefited from an increase in revenue per equivalent admission. Driven in large part by a rise in inpatient revenue per admission, the company saw an increase in revenue per equivalent admission from $17,149 to $18,407. This certainly played a material role in boosting overall sales. And as revenue for the company increased, profitability rose also. Net profits, as an example, jumped from $5.24 billion to $6.78 billion. Adjusted net profits expanded from $5.25 billion to $6.76 billion. We saw operating cash flow grow from $9.43 billion to $12.64 billion. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a jump from $9.99 billion to $12.22 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $12.73 billion to $15.57 billion.

As the company has expanded, management has made some interesting choices. The firm has, for years now, paid out dividends. But those have always been quite small. Over the last three years, it has paid out only $2.03 billion to shareholders this way. I know that to some investors that might seem disappointing. But not to me. I actually prefer a focus on growth and share buyback opportunities. And according to the data provided, the company has achieved not only growth but has also accomplished significant share buybacks. In fact, it went from buying back $3.81 billion worth of shares in 2023 to buying back $10.07 billion in 2025. Over the last three years, the total value of share buybacks amounted to $19.92 billion. This totaled 59 million shares of stock.

Now normally, when I see this kind of buyback activity, I do become concerned about the prospect that the business in question might be taking on too much leverage. But that doesn't appear to be the case here. If anything, the growth in EBITDA has been strong enough to cause overall leverage to drop even as net debt increased. From 2023 through 2025, net debt expanded from $38.66 billion to $45.45 billion. But the net leverage ratio for the company ticked down slightly from 3.04 to 2.92. So if anything, the company is getting healthier, even if only marginally so.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management expects even more good times. They anticipate that revenue will grow to between $76.5 billion and $80 billion. Even the low end of this range would be up from the $75.6 billion the company reported last year. This is not all, though. Net profits should come in at between $6.495 billion and $7.035 billion. At the midpoint, this would mean profitability that is virtually flat. However, EBITDA is forecasted to rise from $15.57 billion to $16 billion year over year. This would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of around $12.56 billion.

If we use these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. These are actually pretty attractive multiples if you ask me. Though relative to other similar firms, the stock is closer to being in the fair value range. Three of the five companies I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis. Two of them were cheaper on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. Three of them, meanwhile, ended up being cheaper on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, while another was basically tied with it.

Even though the relative valuation of the company indicates that I should be more neutral than bullish, I cannot help but to remain optimistic considering all of the facts that I covered already. Meaningful share buybacks at a low multiple, continued growth, and a reduction in leverage look beautiful. But that doesn't mean that my opinion cannot change as new data justifies it. As I mentioned at the outset of this article, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year before the market opens on April 24th. The current expectation is that revenue will come in at $19.07 billion. That would be up nicely from the $18.32 billion that the company reported a year earlier. Earnings per share, meanwhile, should grow from $6.45 to $7.23. That would mean a jump in net income from $1.61 billion to $1.73 billion. Meanwhile, adjusted earnings per share should grow from $6.45 to $7.15. That would translate to a rise in adjusted net income from $1.61 billion to $1.71 billion. The table below also shows certain cash flow metrics for the first quarter of last year. If revenue and profitability both increase, these cash flow figures almost certainly will also.

There are a couple of other things that investors should keep an eye out for when new data does come out. The first is that my estimates assume that the number of shares outstanding for the company will remain unchanged compared to what they were at the end of last year. However, the company did, in their final quarter earnings, announce a new $10 billion share buyback program, plus they raised the dividend from $0.72 to $0.78 each quarter. So we could see from this a further reduction in the number of shares outstanding. The other thing that investors should be paying attention to would be certain operational changes that the company has exhibited.

Even though management acknowledged certain uncertainties, such as the expired and enhanced premium tax credits, as well as certain issues regarding Medicaid supplemental payment programs and the Rural Health Transformation program, they stressed that their core business remains strong and that they should see a 2% to 3% annual growth in volume moving forward.

They also said that they continue to benefit from investments in technology, digital transformation, and operational improvements, all aimed at not only boosting revenue but also achieving specific margin targets. But the resiliency initiatives that the company is working on will face challenges from some of the aforementioned issues that I mentioned already. One good example that I did not mention would be the pause of the Texas Medicaid supplemental payment program that already contributed to a $250 million to $450 million expected decline in net benefits to the business. It's not unthinkable that, as market conditions continue to evolve, the firm could face some challenges here.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my belief that HCA Healthcare remains a relatively attractive prospect. The easy money has certainly been made, but management is doing a fine job even as uncertainty grows. I do think that investors would be wise to keep a close eye as new results roll in. But as the picture stands right now, I am comfortable enough to maintain a soft ‘buy’ rating on the stock.

data.title: Elanco Animal Health: Relying On Innovation To Drive Growth
data.description:
Shares of Elanco Animal Health Incorporated (ELAN) have been settling around the $25 mark after the company really saw its performance come through last year, driven by a focus on internal innovation.

In November I concluded what a difference and innovation a year made, as organic growth returned, convincingly driven by a line-up of innovative products, making those leverage concerns move to the background. That innovative growth is continuing and accelerating, yet it results in relatively modest earnings growth, which makes me a bit cautious here.

Comforting is that sales and margins are seen accelerating in the years to come, even seen accelerating, but that requires real execution, making me a bit reserved here on the valuation at current levels.

Other interesting IPOs are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

A Very Strong Year

In February, Elanco reported a 6% increase in full-year sales to $4.71 billion, with organic growth rates coming in a point higher. Fourth quarter report sales rose as much as 12%, with organic growth accelerating to 9%. I like the composition of organic growth, driven by volumes, which rose by 8% amidst just a percent increase in pricing. Some $892 million of these revenues were classified as so-called innovation revenues.

While the topline sales trends look quite encouraging, adjusted earnings of $473 million were up just $21 million on the year before, with adjusted earnings growth trailing topline sales momentum. This made it so that adjusted earnings rose by three cents to $0.94 per share.

Adjusted EBITDA was reported at $901 million for the year, as leverage ratios were reported at 3.6 times. Adjusted EBITDA was actually down $9 million, driven by the divestment of the aqua business, as comparables from hereon get easier.

The company guided for total sales to advance to $4.95-$5.02 billion in 2026, with innovation sales seen up to $1.15 billion. This suggests just over a quarter of a billion increase in sales in this category, which reveals that the remainder of the portfolio sees flattish sales. Sales growth and the lapsing of the aqua divestment should make it so that EBITDA will increase to $970 million, plus or minus $15 million. This should allow adjusted earnings to increase to $1.03 per share, with net leverage seen down to 3.2 times by year-end.

Some Perspective

With shares trading around the $23 mark, shares of Elanco trade around 23 times adjusted earnings going forward, all while organic growth is solid, yet still some debt is employed.

By now shares are back to the levels last seen in the spring of 2022. Shares came into free fall at the time, following a 2020 deal to merge with Bayer's animal health business. That mega deal followed the 2018 separation of Elanco, when the company was spun off from Eli Lilly (LLY). This company was inspired by a successful spin-off of Zoetis (ZTS) by Pfizer (PFE) a couple of years before.

The Bayer deal was set to add $1.7 billion in sales, valued at around 4.5 times sales, resulting in demanding valuations, as the integration and organic growth have been lackluster ever since. The debt overhang and soft performance made the shares fall to the $10 mark in 2023 and 2025, followed by a recovery ever since.

An encouraging news event was a $1.3 billion sale of the aqua business last year, but more helpful was an increase in the momentum of the innovation brands, which showed more impressive revenue growth.

Getting Better

The reality is that Elanco is doing a lot better, as the question remains how much of a recovery has now been priced in. Trading at a low-20 times earnings multiple, valuations represent a premium, also given the debt employed, as investors like the mid- to high-single-digit organic growth trajectory.

This is driven by innovative brands, including a rapid increase in sales of Credelio Quattro, which reached a $100 million annual sales number just eight months after its launch, as reported in the autumn of last year. More upbeat news on the pipeline was announced, including Zenrelia and others. Growth is driven by the "Big 6," which comprise the aforementioned drugs, AdTab, Befrena, Bovaer, and Experior. The innovation revenues are seen comfortably above a billion in 2026, seen doubling by 2028.

Despite the momentum, EBITDA margins and, therefore, profit margins are barely seen outpacing reported sales growth. This makes me a bit reserved, while the 2028 guidance is rather convincing. With innovation revenues seen doubling through that year, absent of any growth in the remaining portfolio, revenues should come in around $6 billion at such point. Some 200-350 basis points of EBITDA improvements are projected, as margins might improve to 21.0%-22.5% of sales in such a case.

At the midpoint, that might result in a $1.3 billion EBITDA number. Absent of real growth in business and thus depreciating expenses, the company could see about $300 million in pre-tax earnings improvements. Such improvements could easily push up earnings to $1.50 per share at that point, creating a real roadmap for even accelerating earnings growth here.

This is encouraging, and based on this number, shares trade at 16 times forward earnings, about two years in time. That could easily result in further upward pressure on the share price, but it requires real work and accelerating earnings growth in 2027 and 2028 compared to the current trends.

Notably, this expected and even acceleration-guided force makes me feel a bit uneasy. Given all this, I am glad to see the company having turned the corner, making me a happy holder of a tiny long position but unwilling to add here.
